meta.title: VIP Interneurons in Cortical Computation: From Molecular Identity to Circuit Function

central_argument_arc.thesis_question: How do VIP-expressing GABAergic interneurons — defined by CGE lineage and a distinctive transcriptomic, morphological, and physiological profile — implement disinhibitory and gain-modulatory operations that shape cortical computation, and to what extent does the canonical 'VIP disinhibitor' framing survive scrutiny under contemporary multi-modal evidence?
central_argument_arc.thesis_statement: VIP-expressing interneurons are a CGE-derived, transcriptomically heterogeneous GABAergic class whose canonical role as cortical 'disinhibitors' captures a real and reproducible motif but is, on contemporary multi-modal evidence, an incomplete description: VIP cells implement context-, area-, and state-dependent operations — including direct inhibition of pyramidal neurons, parallel control of multiple interneuron classes, and oscillatory and developmental scaffolding functions — that the simple VIP→SST→Pyr motif cannot fully explain.
central_argument_arc.narrative_spine: The review is organised as a three-act argument. Act 1 establishes what a VIP interneuron *is* across molecular, developmental, morphological, and intrinsic axes; Act 2 lays out the empirical complications — synaptic targets that include PV and pyramidal cells, motif diversity, in-vivo response heterogeneity across areas/behaviors, oscillatory and species/disease evidence — that erode a strict disinhibitor reading; Act 3 uses computational models and cross-cluster synthesis to recast VIP cells as multifunctional state- and context-gated controllers and to inventory open questions.

acts.label: Establishment: What is a VIP interneuron?
acts.purpose: Define the class across molecular, developmental, morphological, and intrinsic-physiological axes; introduce the canonical disinhibitor framing that subsequent acts will test.

acts.label: Complications: Connectivity, function, and context
acts.purpose: Surface the body of evidence that VIP function is heterogeneous across cells, motifs, brain regions, behaviors, oscillatory regimes, species, and disease — i.e., the data that complicate a univocal 'disinhibitor' reading.

acts.label: Synthesis & open questions
acts.purpose: Use computational/theoretical models to integrate the empirical strands, then critically reassess the canonical VIP-disinhibitor framing and articulate open questions.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.thesis: VIP-expressing interneurons occupy a distinctive niche in cortical microcircuitry: a numerically small, CGE-derived GABAergic population that has become the canonical example of cortical 'disinhibition' through a stereotyped VIP→SST→pyramidal motif. This review motivates a critical reassessment of that framing, arguing that contemporary multi-modal evidence — transcriptomic, connectomic, in-vivo, and computational — supports a richer view of VIP cells as multifunctional, state- and context-gated regulators of cortical gain.

evidence_anchors.note: Tasic 2018 hierarchical taxonomy showing VIP within CGE branch

conflicts_to_present.nature: Whether the canonical VIP→SST disinhibition motif generalizes across cortical areas and behavioral contexts.

sections.connection_to_next: The Introduction frames the canonical VIP-disinhibitor model and the questions this review will pressure-test; Section 2 begins the test by asking what a VIP cell is at the molecular and transcriptomic level.

sections.title: Molecular Identity and Transcriptomic Taxonomy
sections.thesis: VIP interneurons are a transcriptomically defined GABAergic subclass within the CGE-derived 5-HT3AR lineage, robustly separable from MGE-derived SST and PV classes at the subclass level across modern scRNA-seq atlases, but exhibiting dataset- and species-dependent granularity at the t-type level. Within the VIP subclass, sub-populations including VIP/ChAT, VIP/CR, and VIP/CCK form biologically meaningful axes whose number and discreteness depend on resolution, atlas integration method, and species.

evidence_anchors.note: Tasic 2018 hierarchical dendrogram, 6 GABAergic subclasses, ~14 VIP t-types

Discrete clusters versus continuous transcriptomic gradients within the cortical VIP/CGE compartment.

sections.title: Developmental Origins and Postnatal Maturation
sections.thesis: VIP interneurons originate from caudal ganglionic eminence (CGE) progenitors expressing transcription factors including Prox1, Sp8, and Nr2f2; they migrate tangentially into cortex postnatally and acquire mature electrophysiological and synaptic properties through activity-dependent and intrinsic genetic programs. CGE-lineage proportions and the gene-regulatory logic specifying VIP subclasses are quantitatively divergent between rodent and primate, complicating direct translation of mouse fate-mapping to human.

sections.title: Morphological Diversity
sections.thesis: Cortical VIP cells span a recognisable but graded morphological space dominated by bipolar/bitufted descending-axon morphologies, with smaller proportions of small-basket and arcade-like variants. Patch-seq and large-volume EM reconstructions indicate that subclass-level (VIP vs Sst vs Pvalb) morphological separation is robust, while within-VIP morphological clustering forms a continuum rather than fully discrete classes, with proportions varying across cortical area, layer, and species.

sections.title: Intrinsic Electrophysiology
sections.thesis: VIP interneurons exhibit a characteristic but heterogeneous intrinsic-firing repertoire dominated by irregular-spiking (IS) and continuous-adapting types, with high input resistance, modest sag/Ih, and fast-rising APs. Patch-seq integration shows that intrinsic-firing categories map imperfectly onto transcriptomic t-types: subclass-level features are well-predicted, but within-VIP e-types overlap, and individual properties (resting Vm, IS fraction, mechanism of irregularity) vary across studies and recording conditions.

sections.title: Synaptic Properties and Connectivity
sections.thesis: VIP interneurons receive strong long-range cortico-cortical and thalamic input, modest local excitatory input, and powerful neuromodulatory (cholinergic, serotonergic, noradrenergic) drive; they output preferentially onto SST cells but also non-trivially onto PV and, in some preparations, directly onto pyramidal neurons. The widespread textbook claim that 'VIP cells inhibit only other interneurons' is empirically conditional — quantitatively contested across anatomical and functional studies and across cortical areas — and represents the first major divergence between canonical schematic and contemporary data.

sections.title: Local Circuit Motifs and the Disinhibition Framework
sections.thesis: The VIP→SST→pyramidal disinhibition motif is reproducible and behaviourally engaged in multiple cortical preparations, but it is one of several VIP-mediated motifs — including direct VIP→PV, VIP→Pyr, and VIP→NDNF circuits — that operate in parallel and whose relative weights depend on cortical area, layer, and behavioural state. The canonical motif is best read as a privileged but non-exclusive channel for state-dependent gain, not a defining function of the class.

sections.title: In Vivo Function During Behavior
sections.thesis: In awake animals, VIP cells are reliably recruited by locomotion, arousal, and reinforcement signals, and their activation is causally linked to gain enhancement in V1 and to attentional and learning-related disinhibition in multiple cortices. However, in-vivo VIP activity is far from uniform: response polarity, sensory selectivity, and behavioural correlates differ across cortical area, task, and individual VIP subtype, and the inferred SST-suppression mechanism does not consistently obtain (e.g., concurrent SST activation during locomotion in some preparations).

sections.title: VIP Interneurons Across Brain Regions
sections.thesis: VIP function is area-specific: V1 emphasises locomotion-gated gain enhancement, auditory cortex shows the opposite locomotion polarity, mPFC/ACC implement top-down control of attention and salience, S1 supports whisker-context-dependent disinhibition, and hippocampal VIP/IS interneurons (CA1 IS3 cells, etc.) implement specialised motifs distinct from neocortex. These cross-area differences place strong constraints on any 'unitary' theory of VIP function.

sections.title: Oscillatory Dynamics and Temporal Coordination
sections.thesis: VIP cells contribute to cortical and hippocampal rhythms primarily as state-gating modulators rather than as primary rhythm generators: they bias gamma and theta power through SST/PV control, are recruited differentially during Up-states, REM, and arousal, and are causally implicated in seizure susceptibility in some preparations. Causal attribution is contested: bidirectional perturbation studies disagree on whether VIP cells are necessary, sufficient, or merely permissive for oscillatory state changes.

sections.title: Species Differences, Human Relevance, and Disease
sections.thesis: Subclass-level VIP identity is conserved between mouse and human cortex, but cell-type proportions, t-type granularity, and gene-expression profiles diverge non-trivially: human cortex shows expanded CGE-derived diversity and human-specific transcriptomic features. VIP-IN dysfunction is implicated across Alzheimer's disease, autism-spectrum and intellectual-disability syndromes (Mecp2/Tcf4/Fmr1/Cntnap2/Scn1a), schizophrenia, and Dravet syndrome, but mouse-model phenotypes are heterogeneous and translation to human pathology is non-trivial.

Whether VIP-IN populations are vulnerable or resilient in Alzheimer's disease. Mouse-model evidence reports altered firing/connectivity without overt VIP cell loss, while late-stage human postmortem snRNA-seq describes a depletion of VIP su…

Whether VIP-IN dysfunction in monogenic autism/ID models is a generalised phenotype or a model- and circuit-specific defect. Some Tcf4/Mecp2/Cntnap2 studies describe global VIP excitability changes; Fmr1 studies report layer-, age-, or regi…

Translatability of mouse VIP-IN phenotypes to human iPSC-derived models: human iPSC studies frequently emphasise transcriptional/morphological phenotypes whereas mouse studies emphasise intrinsic excitability and synaptic transmission, with…
Whether mouse VIP/CGE transcriptomic taxonomy is directly transferable to human cortex (homology) or whether human VIP populations require species-specific subtype definitions (divergence).

sections.title: Computational Models of VIP Circuit Function
sections.thesis: Computational models of cortical microcircuits — biophysical, rate-based, and machine-learning-inspired — converge on VIP cells as a flexible gain-control and context-gating element rather than a unitary 'disinhibitor.' Three-interneuron (PV+SST+VIP) architectures support both subtractive and divisive gain regimes depending on connectivity weights, drive, and SST/PV nonlinearities; recent expansions add NDNF and Lamp5 cells and credit-assignment / predictive-coding interpretations. Models are constrained by, and expose, the empirical conflicts of §6–§9: which architectural primitive is necessary remains an open theoretical question.

sections.title: Synthesis and Conclusion: Reassessing the Canonical VIP Disinhibitor
sections.thesis: The canonical 'VIP disinhibitor' framing survives as a useful first-order approximation but should be replaced, in advanced treatments, by a multifunctional view: VIP cells implement context-, area-, layer-, and modulator-dependent gain, gating, and timing operations whose unifying property is the integration of long-range and neuromodulatory drive into local-circuit control. This section consolidates the evidence and inventories the highest-priority open questions — human-specific subtypes, t-type-resolved function, cross-area motif diversity, and disease-relevant translation — that the next decade of VIP research must address.

recurring_themes.name: Disinhibition motif: canonical vs revised
recurring_themes.description: The thread that unifies the review: the textbook VIP→SST→pyramidal disinhibition motif is reproducible but not exhaustive of VIP function. Each section asks how its evidence supports, complicates, or extends the canonical reading.

recurring_themes.name: CGE lineage and cell-type identity
recurring_themes.description: VIP cells are members of the CGE-derived 5-HT3AR+ lineage that also contains Lamp5 and Sncg subclasses; lineage logic recurs in molecular, developmental, morphological, and cross-species treatments.

recurring_themes.name: Modulatory state and context dependence
recurring_themes.description: Cholinergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic drive — together with behavioural state (locomotion, arousal, attention) — gates VIP recruitment, and many empirical conflicts dissolve when modulator context is taken into account.

recurring_themes.name: Discrete-vs-continuum cell-type structure
recurring_themes.description: Whether within-VIP variation is best modelled as discrete t-types/MET-types or as continuous gradients is a recurring methodological question that appears at every level (transcriptomic, morphological, electrophysiological, cross-species).

recurring_themes.name: Area- and species-specificity
recurring_themes.description: VIP function is not uniform across cortical areas (V1 vs A1 vs PFC vs S1) or across species (mouse vs primate vs human), and these differences constrain any unitary theory.

recurring_themes.name: Methodological caveats: VIP-Cre line, ChAT subset, modality choice
recurring_themes.description: Many cross-study conflicts trace to driver-line off-targets, anatomical-vs-functional methodological mismatches, and species-specific marker reliability — flagged explicitly throughout.

terminology_conventions.VIP+ vs VIP: Use 'VIP+' when referring to cells immunopositive for VIP peptide / VIP-Cre-labelled; use 'VIP cells' or 'VIP interneurons' when referring to the transcriptomic subclass irrespective of expression. Avoid bare 'VIP' as a noun for the cells.
terminology_conventions.SST vs SOM: Use 'SST' (somatostatin) for both peptide and cell-class throughout; reserve 'SOM' only when quoting a paper's nomenclature directly.
terminology_conventions.PV vs PV+: 'PV' for the subclass; 'PV+' when referring to immunolabelled cells specifically.
terminology_conventions.M cells / IS cells: Use 'irregular-spiking (IS)' for the firing class. 'IS3 cells' is the established name for hippocampal CA1 VIP/CR/CCK cells described by Tyan/Topolnik.
terminology_conventions.gene symbols: Italicise mouse gene symbols (e.g., *Vip*, *Chat*, *Sst*); proteins / peptides are non-italic and uppercase (VIP, ChAT, SST).
terminology_conventions.CGE vs MGE: Always uppercase, never expanded after first use.
terminology_conventions.ChAT+ VIP: Refer to 'VIP/ChAT' cells (slash) for the subclass; 'ChAT+ VIP' (plus) when describing the immunolabelled subset.
terminology_conventions.cite-keys: All citations use authoryear cite-keys from citation_key_map.json (e.g., Tasic2018, Pi2013). Multi-author papers: first-author surname + year. Suffix letters (a/b/c) are reserved for first-author/year collisions and must match the map verbatim.

voice_and_tone: Past tense for empirical findings (e.g., 'Pi et al. (2013) showed that VIP activation disinhibited Pyr neurons').
Present tense for theoretical claims and accepted background ('VIP cells are CGE-derived').
Hedge probabilistic conclusions ('the data are consistent with', 'most studies report', 'in the majority of preparations'); never overclaim from single studies.
When findings conflict, state both positions explicitly using the {admonition} Conflict block — do not paper over disagreement.
Adopt a critical-reassessment stance toward the canonical disinhibitor framing without polemic; describe canonical readings respectfully and locate their empirical limits.
Avoid evaluative adjectives ('elegant', 'beautiful', 'striking'); prefer quantitative or structural descriptors.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: Number of cortical VIP transcriptomic types (Tasic2016 ~5 vs Tasic2018 ~14)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Resolution depends on clustering resolution and dataset; subclass-level robust, t-type-level dataset-dependent. State both numbers, present resolution explicitly.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: Whether VIP boutons target only interneurons (Pfeffer2013) or also pyramidal cells (Zhou2017 et al.)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Direct VIP→Pyr connections exist but are minor/area-conditional; canonical 'IN-only' textbook claim is approximation. Spell out where each finding holds.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: Locomotion mechanism — Fu2014 (VIP→SST disinhibition) vs Pakan2016 (concurrent SST activation)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Both findings reproduce in their respective preparations; resolution likely involves cell-type subset, layer, and behavioural-context differences. Flag as canonical motif's most-discussed empirical limit.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: VIP disinhibition as privileged channel vs one of parallel inhibitory streams (Pi2013 vs Kuchibhotla2017)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Treat as the central conflict animating the review's reassessment thesis. Present both with explicit weighing.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: Generalisation of VIP→SST motif across cortical areas (Lee2013 vs Pakan2016)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Motif obtains in V1/PFC under specific contexts; A1 and certain hippocampal preparations show different polarity. Map area × condition matrix in §9.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: SST inhibition: subtractive vs divisive gain control (multiple models vs Wilson2012)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Both regimes are recoverable in 3-IN models depending on parameters and dendritic nonlinearities; present as parameter-dependent, not categorical.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: VIP cell preservation vs loss in Alzheimer's disease (mouse models vs human postmortem; Yi2025a vs Gabitto2024)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Mouse models report functional but not numerical loss; human snRNA-seq shows subtype depletion. Translation gap explicitly flagged as open question.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: Mouse VIP/CGE taxonomy transferable to human cortex (Hodge2019) vs species-specific divergence (Mayer2018)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Subclass conserved, t-type partially divergent, integration-algorithm-dependent. State degree of conservation explicitly per modality.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: Bidirectional VIP perturbation effects on seizure activity (Khoshkhoo2017 silencing vs Calin2018 activation)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Asymmetric effects of activation vs silencing across models; flag as unresolved with proposed reconciliations.

conflict_resolution_roadmap.nature: % of cortical GABAergic neurons attributable to CGE/CR+ lineage (mouse ~30% vs human ~50%)
conflict_resolution_roadmap.stance: Quantitative species difference; present as a real biological expansion of the CGE lineage in primates rather than methodological discrepancy.

panels: Panel A: Forest plot of VIP→SST IPSC amplitude (paired-recording or optogenetic CRACM) across studies (axis: pA, mean ± SD).

Panel C: Bar chart of VIP-activation-evoked Pyr firing-rate change (subtractive, divisive, net-disinhibitory) sorted by cortical area and behavioural state.
Panel D: Heat-map mapping conflict pairs (paper_a vs paper_b cite-keys) onto Δ effect direction, providing a 'conflict atlas' for §7 prose.

figure_roadmap.label: fig-vip-behavior
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: VIP recruitment is reliable for locomotion/arousal/reinforcement in V1, but heterogeneous across area and task; the SST-suppression mechanism does not always obtain.
panels: Panel A: Across-study compilation of locomotion-evoked ΔF/F in V1 VIP (axis: % change from baseline; mean ± SEM per study).
Panel B: Heat-map of VIP response polarity across cortices (V1, S1, A1, ACC, mPFC, M1) for locomotion, attention, reward, punishment.
Panel C: Concurrent VIP/SST imaging traces from contrasting studies (Fu 2014 vs Pakan 2016 vs Dipoppa 2018).
Panel D: Schematic decision diagram for resolving area×task×modulator → expected VIP polarity.

figure_roadmap.label: fig-vip-state-decision-tree
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: Decision schematic for predicting in-vivo VIP polarity from cortical area, behavioural state, and dominant neuromodulator — operationalising the section's claim that area×state×modulator interactions resolve apparent disagreements.
panels: Panel A: Decision tree: cortical area (V1/A1/S1/PFC/hippocampus) → state (locomotion/quiescence/attention/reward) → dominant modulator (ACh/5-HT/NA) → predicted VIP response sign.
Panel B: Annotated overlay marking which leaves of the tree each cardinal study (Fu2014, Pakan2016, Pi2013, Lee2013, Dipoppa2018, Pakan2016, Vervaeke2024) populates.
Panel C: Cartoon of the mouse running on a wheel with V1/A1/S1 sketches showing concurrent expected VIP-vs-SST polarity per area (referencing §9 for follow-up).

figure_roadmap.label: fig-vip-across-areas
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: VIP recruits opposite-sign motifs in V1 vs A1 and instantiates distinct circuits in hippocampus.
figure_roadmap.data_source: cluster_08_brain_regions evidence + cluster_09_oscillations.conflicts (V1 vs A1 polarity)
panels: Panel A: Brain-area schematic with arrows summarising dominant VIP motif per area (V1, A1, S1, M1, ACC, mPFC, CA1, BLA).
Panel B: Bar chart of locomotion-evoked VIP response sign in V1 vs A1 (axis: ΔF/F).
Panel C: Hippocampal VIP/IS3 motif schematic (VIP/CR/CCK distinctions; OLM/CA1 PYR targeting).
Panel D: Cross-area conflict table (top-down vs neuromodulatory vs sensory drivers).

figure_roadmap.label: fig-hippocampal-vip-motif
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: Hippocampal CA1 VIP/CR/CCK 'IS3' cells implement a circuit motif distinct from neocortex (preferential targeting of OLM cells; reset of theta-coupled inhibition) that contradicts a uniform cross-area VIP function and motivates separate modelling.
panels: Panel A: CA1 lamellar cartoon with VIP/IS3 cell soma in stratum oriens, axonal projections onto OLM and bistratified cells, and modulation of CA1 Pyr by disinhibition of distal-dendritic input.
Panel B: Comparison schematic of VIP cells in cortex (bipolar, descending; targets SST in L5) vs hippocampus (IS3; targets OLM in oriens).
Panel C: Subcortical VIP pathways (BLA, striatum, hypothalamus) summarised as a side panel for cross-region completeness.

figure_roadmap.label: fig-vip-oscillations
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: VIP shapes but does not generate cortical rhythms; perturbation effects are direction- and model-dependent.
panels: Panel A: Spectrogram exemplars of cortical LFP during VIP optogenetic activation vs silencing.
Panel B: Forest plot of VIP-perturbation effects on gamma/theta power across studies.
Panel C: Cross-study seizure-susceptibility table (silencing/activating VIP × outcome).
Panel D: VIP recruitment during Up-states and REM (in-vivo summary).

figure_roadmap.label: fig-vip-oscillation-mechanism
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: Mechanistic schematic of how VIP→SST and VIP→PV motifs differentially shape gamma vs theta rhythms, providing the conceptual bridge between the connectivity data of §6–§7 and the LFP/oscillatory observations summarised in this section's data panel.
panels: Panel A: Cartoon circuit diagram of PV-driven gamma rhythm with VIP-mediated SST suppression; predicted effect of VIP activation on gamma power and Pyr phase locking.
Panel B: Cartoon of theta-rhythm circuit (hippocampal/cortical) with VIP/IS3 cell phase-coupled to OLM disinhibition.
Panel C: Schematic of cortical Up-state with VIP as a permissive vs causal gate (referencing Neske2016 vs Pi2013 conflict).

figure_roadmap.label: fig-species-disease
figure_roadmap.section_title: Species Differences, Human Relevance, and Disease
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: Subclass-level VIP conserved across mouse/human; t-type and disease-relevant phenotypes diverge; multiple disorders converge on VIP-IN dysfunction.
panels: Panel A: Mouse vs human VIP transcriptomic-class side-by-side dendrogram.
Panel B: Cross-disease phenotype matrix (AD, ASD/ID, schizophrenia, Dravet × cell-loss / activity / connectivity / morphology).
Panel C: Bar chart of CGE/CR+ fraction of cortical GABA across mouse, marmoset, macaque, human.
Panel D: Convergence diagram — upstream regulators (MeCP2, TCF4, FMR1, SCN1A, CNTNAP2) → VIP-IN phenotypes → circuit-level outcomes.

figure_roadmap.label: fig-vip-disease-convergence
figure_roadmap.section_title: Species Differences, Human Relevance, and Disease
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: Convergence schematic for upstream genetic/cellular insults that funnel into shared VIP-IN dysfunction phenotypes across psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and neurodegenerative disease models — making explicit the translational hypotheses that the species/disease data panel only summarises piecewise.
panels: Panel A: Funnel diagram from upstream regulators (MeCP2, TCF4, FMR1, SCN1A, CNTNAP2, APP/PSEN, DISC1) → mid-level mechanisms (excitability, connectivity, density) → circuit phenotype (E/I imbalance, gain dysfunction).
Panel B: Cartoon comparison of mouse-model vs human-postmortem vs iPSC-derived readout for each disease (highlighting where translation succeeds vs fails).
Panel C: Mouse-vs-human VIP-IN cartoon (relative density, gene-expression overlap, primate-expanded subtypes) anchored to Hodge2019/Yao2023 atlases.

figure_roadmap.label: fig-computational-models
figure_roadmap.section_title: Computational Models of VIP Circuit Function
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: Three-IN models and their extensions support multiple gain regimes; the necessity of any single architectural element is contested.
panels: Panel A: Side-by-side schematics of canonical 3-IN (Hertag/Cornford), 4-IN (NDNF), and predictive-coding/credit-assignment architectures.
Panel B: Phase-diagram of subtractive-vs-divisive gain regime as a function of VIP→SST and SST→Pyr weights.
Panel C: Comparison table — model × interneuron classes × VIP function (gain, attention, prediction error, learning gate).
Panel D: Mapping of empirical conflicts (§6–§9) onto model parameter regimes that resolve them.

figure_roadmap.label: fig-vip-model-empirical-mapping
figure_roadmap.section_title: Computational Models of VIP Circuit Function
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: Mapping schematic linking specific empirical conflicts from §6–§9 to the model parameter regimes that resolve them — reframing controversies as parameter-region rather than categorical disputes, which is the section's synthetic claim.
panels: Panel A: 2D phase diagram (axes: VIP→SST weight × SST→Pyr dendritic nonlinearity) with shaded regions corresponding to subtractive, divisive, and paradoxical-inhibition regimes; named studies plotted at their inferred location.
Panel B: Tree of model architectural choices (3-IN vs 4-IN with NDNF; rate vs spiking; predictive-coding embedding) annotated with which §6–§9 conflicts each architecture predicts vs cannot resolve.
Panel C: Schematic of identifiability problem — model parameter symmetries that prevent existing data from constraining VIP function uniquely (motivates the §13 open-question agenda).

figure_roadmap.label: fig-synthesis-grid
figure_roadmap.section_title: Synthesis and Conclusion: Reassessing the Canonical VIP Disinhibitor
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: VIP function is multifunctional and context-dependent; the canonical motif is one (privileged) channel.
panels: Panel A: 'Canonical-vs-revised' two-column figure: canonical VIP→SST→Pyr motif on the left; revised multifunctional view (parallel motifs, modulator-gated, area-specific) on the right.
Panel B: Open-question heat-map (rows: open question; columns: §2–§12 sections that bear on it) marking high-priority gaps.
Panel C: Translational outlook diagram (mouse-model → human cortex → disease) flagging where translation is supported vs unresolved.

figure_roadmap.label: fig-vip-replication-strength-matrix
figure_roadmap.section_title: Synthesis and Conclusion: Reassessing the Canonical VIP Disinhibitor
figure_roadmap.argument_supported: Cross-cluster replication-strength matrix consolidating which canonical claims about VIP cells are robustly replicated, which are contested, and which remain unresolved — the empirical inventory that backs the section's reassessment of the canonical motif.
figure_roadmap.data_source: Synthesis across replicated_claims and contested_claims fields of all 14 clusters in phase4_scaffold_input.json (cluster_01 through cluster_13_cge_lineage_context).
panels: Panel A: Heat-map (rows: cardinal claims — 'CGE origin', 'VIP→SST functional', 'VIP→PV functional', 'VIP→Pyr direct', 'V1 locomotion gain', 'A1 locomotion suppression', 'cross-species subclass conservation', 'AD VIP loss', etc.; columns: clusters/sections; cells: green=replicated, amber=contested, grey=unresolved).
Panel B: Bar chart of #replicated vs #contested vs #unresolved claims per section, ordered by act.
Panel C: Open-question priority list — top 8 unresolved questions weighted by number of contributing clusters and translational impact.

heading_hierarchy: # Section title (one per section, matches scope.sections[i].title)
#### Use sparingly; prefer paragraph subheading via bold lead-in
style_guide.citation_style: Use MyST cite-key references inline, e.g. {cite}`Tasic2018` or {cite}`Pi2013,Lee2013,Fu2014`. All cite-keys must come from citation_key_map.json verbatim. Do not write '(Tasic et al. 2018)' or '[1]' inline; the bibliography assembler does that.
style_guide.figure_inclusion:
Use MyST colon-fence figure blocks ONLY (never reST or HTML):
    :::{figure} fig-vip-taxonomy.svg
    :name: fig-vip-taxonomy
    :width: 80%
    Caption text. {cite}`Tasic2018`
    :::
Cross-reference figures via {numref}`fig-vip-taxonomy` or {ref}`fig-vip-taxonomy`.
style_guide.cross_reference_format: Use {ref}`sec-LABEL` for section cross-references (e.g. {ref}`sec-disinhibition-framework`). Slugs are listed in scaffold.sections[*].cross_ref_label and are AUTHORITATIVE — do not invent new ones.
style_guide.conflict_admonition:
Whenever two studies disagree, surface the conflict in a colon-fence admonition:
    :::{admonition} Conflict
    :class: conflict
    {cite}`Pfeffer2013` reported X, while {cite}`Zhou2017` reported Y. Resolution: ... (or 'unresolved').
    :::
Do not bury conflicts in body prose; use the admonition block for every entry in the section's conflicts_to_present list.
tense_conventions: Past tense for empirical findings (e.g., 'Pi et al. activated VIP cells and observed...').
Present tense for accepted background and theoretical claims ('VIP cells *are* CGE-derived', 'the canonical motif *predicts* gain enhancement').
Hedged present for active controversies ('it remains unclear whether...', 'the data are consistent with...').
numerical_style: Effect sizes: report units and direction explicitly (e.g., 'increased ΔF/F by 30 ± 8%').
Sample sizes: state n per condition when interpreting an effect ('n = 12 cells / 4 mice').
Use SI prefixes; italicise scalar variables (n, *t*, *F*, *r*).
Confidence intervals or SEM/SD must be specified — never bare '±'.
writing_constraints: Section thesis paragraphs lead each section; do not use abstracts.
Each subsection that surfaces a conflict must include the {admonition} Conflict block.
Cap paragraph length at ~200 words; break for readability.
Avoid speculative claims unless explicitly flagged 'we speculate' / 'remains an open question'.
Cross-reference forward and backward generously — readers should be able to enter at any section.
